scenario: 🏨 Hotel English — Checking In: 'I have a reservation under...'
text:
🎧 YouGlish
At check-in, tell the receptionist: {{c1::"Hi, I have a reservation under the name Colin."}}
explanation:
Native speed: 'haɪ, aɪ hæ·və rɛz·ər·veɪ·ʃən ʌn·dər ðə neɪm...'
Rules applied:
• have a → /hæ·və/ (C+V linking + schwa)
• reservation under → /rɛz·ər·veɪ·ʃə·nʌn·dər/ (linking final /n/ to /ʌ/)

🧠 'Under the name [X]' is the standard linguistic pattern for reservations in hotels, restaurants, and event bookings.
usage:
• "I have a reservation under the name Colin, check-in for three nights."
  • "Could you look up a reservation under 'Colin'?"
spanish: 'Hola, tengo una reservación a nombre de Colin.' — El patrón 'under the name [X]' es la estructura por excelencia para indicar a nombre de quién está la reserva. Fonéticamente, 'have a' y 'reservation under' se entrelazan de manera continua.

scenario: 🏨 Hotel English — Requesting Amenities: 'Is breakfast included?'
text:
🎧 YouGlish
To clarify hotel services, ask: {{c1::"Is breakfast included, and what's the Wi-Fi password?"}}
explanation:
Native speed: 'ɪz brɛk·fəs·tɪn·kluː·dɪd, ən wʌ·sðə waɪ·faɪ pæs·wərd?'
• breakfast included → /brɛk·fəs·tɪn·kluː·dɪd/ (linking final /t/ of 'breakfast' to initial /ɪ/ of 'included')
• and what's → /ən·wʌts/ (weak /d/ deletion in 'and' + schwa)

🧠 Always ask this at the desk to avoid hidden charges. Note the /t/ migration from 'breakfast' to 'included'.
usage:
• "Is breakfast included in the room rate?"
  • "What time does breakfast start?"
spanish: '¿Está incluido el desayuno, y cuál es la contraseña del Wi-Fi?' — Pregunta esencial de hotel. La /t/ final de 'breakfast' se enlaza directamente con la /ɪ/ de 'included' creando un sonido fluido.

scenario: 🏨 Hotel English — Room Complaints: 'The AC isn't working'
text:
🎧 YouGlish
To report a problem politely but effectively, tell the front desk: {{c1::"Excuse me, the AC in my room isn't working properly."}}
explanation:
Native speed: 'ɪk·skjuːz mi, ði eɪ·siː ɪn maɪ ruːm ɪ·zn̩t wər·kɪŋ prɑː·pər·li'
• isn't → /ɪ·zn̩t/ or /ɪ·zn̩/ (the final /t/ is often elided or glottalized before consonants)
• 'AC' is the most common abbreviation for air conditioning.

🧠 Politeness anchors: Lead with 'Excuse me' to reduce defensiveness in staff.
usage:
• "Excuse me, the heater isn't working properly."
  • "The hot water isn't coming out."
spanish: 'Disculpe, el aire acondicionado de mi habitación no funciona correctamente.' — Informar sobre fallas de forma asertiva. Fonética: la /t/ en 'isn't' generalmente desaparece o se frena antes de 'working'.
